name: Greek-Style Shrimp with Tomatoes and Feta
description: Plump shrimp baked in a garlicky tomato sauce under crumbled feta — a fast Greek one-dish meal; serve over orzo or with crusty bread to catch the sauce.
recipeIngredient: 1.5 lb extra-large shrimp (21 to 25 per pound), peeled and deveined
1 red or green bell pepper, stemmed, seeded, and chopped
1 can diced tomatoes (28 oz), drained with 1/2 cup juice reserved
recipeInstructions: Toss shrimp in a bowl with 1 tablespoon oil, 1 tablespoon ouzo, 1 teaspoon of the garlic, the lemon zest, 1/4 teaspoon salt, and 1/8 teaspoon pepper; set aside.

Heat 2 tablespoons oil in a 12-inch skillet over medium heat until shimmering. Add onion, bell pepper, and 1/4 teaspoon salt; cover and cook, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables release their liquid, 3 to 5 minutes. Uncover and cook until the liquid evaporates and the vegetables soften, about 5 minutes. Stir in the remaining garlic and the pepper flakes and cook until fragrant, about 1 minute.

Stir in the tomatoes and reserved juice, wine, and remaining 2 tablespoons ouzo. Bring to a simmer and cook, stirring occasionally, until the flavors meld and the sauce is slightly thickened (it should not be completely dry), 5 to 8 minutes. Stir in the parsley and season with salt and pepper to taste.

Reduce heat to medium-low and add the shrimp with any accumulated juices; stir to coat and distribute evenly. Cover and cook, stirring occasionally, until the shrimp are opaque throughout, 6 to 9 minutes, adjusting heat to maintain a bare simmer. Off heat, sprinkle with feta and dill and drizzle with the remaining 1 tablespoon oil. Serve.
author: America's Test Kitchen

ai.cliffNotes: Plump shrimp baked in a garlicky tomato sauce under crumbled feta — a fast Greek one-dish meal; serve over orzo or with crusty bread to catch the sauce.
keyTips: Toss the shrimp with ouzo, garlic, and lemon zest first — it infuses them with bright anise flavor while you build the sauce.
Cook the shrimp gently right in the sauce over medium-low at a bare simmer (6 to 9 minutes) so they stay tender instead of turning rubbery.
Keep the sauce slightly saucy — do not cook it completely dry.
No ouzo? Substitute Pernod, or vodka plus 1 teaspoon anise seeds.
Serve over steamed white rice and/or with crusty bread to catch the sauce.
